Hi! I’m Ville, UX designer, author of Foolproof HTML
Hi! I’m Ville, UX designer, author of Foolproof HTML

Thanks, <@U5T9JLUAE>, I had contacted both Dave and Glen before I posted here about it, but haven’t heard back yet. But turns out I know enough people who have connections to it, and bumped into even more people I know when I visited yesterday. Fascinating.

I only had an hour, but already had my mind blown several times, so it’ll take me a while — and another visit — to make up my mind and tell you more about it here.

I’d love to share more info on what the rules are around visiting, but I couldn’t yet speak to somebody official enough about it. There were lots of “volunteers” last night. Apparently, they are trying something they call “community hours”, but it doesn’t appear to be official just yet. I will report as soon as I learn more.

Oh, to be precise: Glen got back to me immediately and recommended another person to contact. I have not heard back from that person yet. (That was less than 24 hours ago, so it’s not as dramatic as it might sound.)

Hey, <@U5TCAFTD3>, I’m working my way through your podcast — thanks for making the effort to record and publish — and in episode #4 when you talk about the “spreadsheets all the way down” idea you say “streams are just spreadsheets”. Can you elaborate on that point a bit more or point me to some resources?
Hey <@U5STGTB3J>! Thanks so much for listening to the podcast! It mean so much to me that you're finding value in it
Anyone used Petri Nets before to model anything? <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Petri_net>

attachments.title: Petri net
attachments.text:
A Petri net, also known as a place/transition (PT) net, is one of several mathematical modeling languages for the description of distributed systems. It is a class of discrete event dynamic system. A Petri net is a directed bipartite graph, in which the nodes represent transitions (i.e. events that may occur, represented by bars) and places (i.e. conditions, represented by circles). The directed arcs describe which places are pre- and/or postconditions for which transitions (signified by arrows). Some sources state that Petri nets were invented in August 1939 by Carl Adam Petri—at the age of 13—for the purpose of describing chemical processes.
Like industry standards such as UML activity diagrams, Business Process Model and Notation and EPCs, Petri nets offer a graphical notation for stepwise processes that include choice, iteration, and concurrent execution. Unlike these standards, Petri nets have an exact mathematical definition of their execution semantics, with a well-developed mathematical theory for process analysis.

text: That's a great question... a lot of thoughts around that project are scattered all over my journal (<http://futureofcoding.org/joural|futureofcoding.org/joural>) but I'm reorganizing website imminently (<https://github.com/stevekrouse/futureofcoding.org/issues/51#issuecomment-334211450>) which should help. In the meanwhile, let me create a github issue for this to start answering your question... <https://github.com/stevekrouse/futureofcoding.org/issues/54>
